title: Former Conservatives joining Reform is not without risk for Farage
content:
How significant is Nadhim Zahawi's decision to join Reform?

The short answer is that it matters – but it's not without risk for Nigel Farage's party.

Let's start with the case for it being an important moment.

Zahawi is probably the most significant figure to make the jump from the Conservatives to Reform.

In government, he helped oversee the widely praised Covid vaccine rollout. He served as education secretary. And, very briefly, he was chancellor and then the most senior minister at the Cabinet Office, the engine room of Whitehall.

Nigel Farage has believed for some months that if Reform are to be ready for power, they need to have senior figures with experience of how government works.

Zahawi ticks a few boxes in that sense.

The former MP also has a compelling back story. He was born in Iraq, but fled with his family when he was a boy.

When he arrived in the UK, he didn't speak English. But since then, he has had a successful career in business, including co-founding the polling firm YouGov. His is the story of an immigrant who has risen to be a senior figure in the business world and government.

Zahawi joins a growing list of senior Conservatives who have defected to Reform. The BBC's latest tally shows 22 ex-MPs who have now made that leap. Among them are a number of former cabinet and junior ministers.

Many of them were among the most vocal supporters of Boris Johnson:Nadine Dorries,Andrea Jenkyns, Jonathan Gullis, Lia Nici, now Zahawi. The list goes on.

For a party seeking to replace the Conservatives as the main force on the right of British politics, the attraction of regular defections is clear.

Here are people who have been at the very top of the Conservative Party, who no longer think it is fit for purpose. They have all concluded there is a need for something new – and have made the jump to Reform.

But this is also where the risks come in.

Reform have been highly critical of the Conservatives in power – not least Boris Johnson's record on issues like immigration.

Many Reform members despise what the Conservative Party did in power and plenty are sceptical about the idea of sharing a party with former cabinet ministers.

You can expect attacks from Reform's political opponents: that the party is increasingly a collection of failed Conservative MPs. The Tories themselves have claimed Reform is becoming a party of "has-been politicians looking for their next gravy train".

It's a risk Nigel Farage has acknowledged. He claims he has turned down other Conservatives who wanted to join his party, but whom he didn't think were suitable.

But the question Farage faces is how many former Tory MPs is too many? Some think he may have already reached the limit.

Then there's another risk: Zahawi himself.

Tonight, the Conservatives have claimed he was lobbying them just a few weeks ago to be made a member of the House of Lords. A source close to Mr Zahawi has denied he "begged" for a peerage – but the Conservatives are accusing their former MP of only making the jump because they wouldn't give him what he wanted.

There's also Zahawi's personal tax affairs. He wasgiven the boot by Rishi Sunak after a highly critical report from the prime minister's ethics adviserwho said he failed to disclose that HMRC were investigating his tax affairs.

This wasn't a small issue – the report found a serious breach of the ministerial code. It was judged that Zahawi had missed multiple chances to be open about his tax affairs. Zahawi called his mistakes careless, but it brought his ministerial career to an ignominious end.

Nigel Farage has decided the baggage is worth it – and that the positives of Nadhim Zahawi's defection outweigh the negatives.

There's no doubt this is another coup which has got Westminster talking – and wondering who might be next.

But the drip drip of former Conservatives joining Reform is not without risk.
